Center: US National Nuclear Data Center, Brookhaven, USA
ref: Jour: Physical Review, Part C, Nuclear Physics, Vol.110, p.035808 (2024)
title: Coincident measurement of the 12C+12C fusion cross section via the differential thick-target technique

x4freetext: Coincident measurement of the 12C+12C fusion cross
section via the differential thick-target technique

x4codes.shortRef: Jour: Physical Review, Part C, Nuclear Physics, Vol.110, p.035808 (2024)

x4codes.hlp: University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, IN, United States of America
Instituto Nacional de Investigaciones Nucleares, Mexico
University of Notre Dame, Notre Dame, IN, United States of America

x4freetext: The single-ended 5U Pelletron
accelerator at the Nuclear Science Laboratory (NSL)
of the University of Notre Dame.
HPGe detector with relative efficiency of
109% (relative to that of 3x3 in. NaI at 1.33 MeV) was
placed in a 10 cm thick lead castle and positioned
right behind the target to maximize the detection
efficiency of gamma rays. The Silicon-Detector Array
at Notre Dame (SAND) for detection of protons and
alpha particles consists of six YY1-type silicon
detectors and one S2-type silicon detector, covering
polar angles from 102 to 146 degrees and 151 to 170
degrees in the laboratory frame.
The target was a highly ordered pyrolytic graphite
(HOPG), which has a layered structure of multiple
thin graphene sheets. The advantage of using HOPG as
target material is its superior purity compared to
natural graphite (e.g., about 1% in impurity of heavy
elements that can cause backscattering of the beams
in an early test). Impurity of hydrogen and
deuterium in the target can cause background in the
charged particle spectra while impurity of 23Na can
severely affect the gamma spectra. The HOPG target
with dimensions of 2 cm x 2 cm x 1mm served also as a
water-cooled beam stop.
Particle-gamma coincidence and differential
thick-target techniques with SAND at the high-intensity
5U Pelletron accelerator.
Statistical errors.
The stopping powers.
Efficiency calibration and summing effects.
Approved by Wanpeng Tan on March 13, 2025.
BP
